title: Simple Faux Fireplace Mantle
summary: A freestanding faux fireplace built from dimensional lumber and a panel of brick paneling. Mostly straight cuts plus one arched header.
description:
This is a fireplace with no fireplace behind it — a freestanding surround you set against a wall to get the mantel, the hearth-side styling, and a place to hang stockings, without any masonry or a real firebox. The whole thing is framed from construction lumber and skinned with a sheet of faux brick paneling, so the material bill is small and every joint is hidden by the time you're done.

Structurally it's close to a full-size headboard, which is not a coincidence — if you've built a farmhouse bed the leg-and-header assembly will look familiar. The only shaped cut is the arch across the top of the 2x12 header; everything else is square. Cut the arch once, sand it fair, and the rest is screwing parts together and covering the fasteners with filler and paint.

The one thing you cannot skip: a freestanding fireplace this tall is top-heavy and will tip forward if a child pulls on the mantel. Screw the header into a wall stud behind it. That single screw is the difference between decor and a hazard.

tools.note: Straight cuts on the 4x4 and 2x stock.
Cuts the arch across the top of the 2x12 header.
Counterbore the screw heads into the 4x4 legs so they sink flush and fill clean.

materials.name: Faux brick paneling, 4x8 sheet (half used)
materials.note: 1/4" plywood or MDF paneling works too — anything for the front face.

materials.name: 4x4 post, 8 ft
materials.note: The two legs. Two 2x4s laminated will substitute if you'd rather.

materials.name: Primer and paint
materials.note: Plus a small can of stain if you want a contrasting mantel top.

cutList.note: Cut the top edge to an arch, then sand it fair.

steps.title: Cut and sand the arched header
steps.body: Lay out an arch across the top edge of the 2x12 header and cut it with a jigsaw. It doesn't have to match a template — a gentle, symmetric curve is the whole goal. Sand the sawn edge until the arc is smooth and even; a wavy jigsaw line reads as sloppy once it's painted a solid color.

steps.title: Attach the inner trim to the header
steps.body:
The 2x4 inner trim pieces screw to the arched header to frame the opening. This joint doesn't need to be strong — it gets reinforced by the legs and back panel later — so don't overthink it. If you have a pocket-hole jig, use it; otherwise just drive screws at an angle from the 2x4 into the 2x12.

Note that 2x12 stock varies in actual width; measure yours rather than trusting the nominal 11-1/4" and adjust the trim placement to suit.

steps.title: Add the 4x4 legs
steps.body: Set the two 4x4 legs against the sides with their backs flush to the back of the assembly. Counterbore each screw hole first: drill partway through the 4x4 with a bit the size of the screw head, then finish the hole with a bit the size of the shaft. That sinks the head below the surface so it fills and paints invisibly. Fasten the legs to the inner trim with 3" screws.

steps.title: Screw on the top rail
steps.body: Screw the 51-1/2" 2x4 top across the tops of the legs with 3" screws. This ties the two legs together and gives the mantel top something to land on. If it looks like a headboard at this stage, that's expected — the frame really is the same idea.

steps.title: Add the mantel top
steps.body: Center the 2x6 mantel top on the top rail with an even overhang front and sides, and screw it down through the 2x4 below. This is the display surface, so keep it flat and its front edge parallel to the wall.

steps.title: Skin the front with paneling
steps.body: Screw the faux brick paneling onto the front to close off the opening and give the fireplace its face. Keep the fasteners near the edges where trim or the frame will hide them.

steps.title: Fill, paint, and anchor to the wall
steps.body:
Fill every screw hole, sand flush, and prime. Paint the frame the color you want and, if you like the contrast, stain the mantel top instead of painting it.

Before you call it done: drive a screw through the header into a wall stud behind the fireplace. A freestanding surround this tall is top-heavy and will tip forward the first time someone leans on the mantel. This is not optional.

images.alt: Freestanding faux fireplace mantle with an arched opening and brick-paneled face
